So the next thing I want to cover is something called pacing and leading, which is an absolutely crucial skill that you must master because it helps you get to that level of unconscious or poor. You can move somebody emotionally without them even knowing it. Now I'll give you an example of pacing and leading here. It's about my son Carter when he was 11 years old. So it's a great story. It takes place five or six years ago and Carter was a great soccer player playing on a club team and there was one kid on his team who was a notorious ball hog and a pusher and a shovel and an elbow. I know Carter didn't really like this kid but one day I came home from work and my partner, my now fiance, she goes, Carter is downstairs and he is so angry and ticked off. He's yelling. He's literally spitting nails. He is so upset about this kid, Andreas. And I'm like, really? It's like, oh my God. He said, all right, let me go down and let me try to talk to my son and try to reason with him. So I go down there and I'm like, hey buddy, what's going on? He's like, no, nothing's going on. I am so angry. I'm so ticked off. This kid, Andreas, he says, calm down Carter. No, I'm not calm down. I'm like, look, you're asking me to calm down in a moment. I guess he just gets so embarrassed. He's ruining my life. I said, Carter, it's not that. It is worse than that. And now on and on it goes. Let me ask you a question. Do you think that myself knowing what I know about human communication, do you think I actually did that? Do you think I went downstairs while my son was in this unbelievably angry ticked off state and I tried to be nice and calm and sweet? Of course I didn't. When you try that with somebody, when you try to enter someone's world where they're not typically they'll even get angrier or they'll go more in the direction where they already are. So I did exactly the opposite. I go down there and do exactly this. I'm like, Carter, what's going on right now? It goes, dad, I am so f*****g off about this kid. I said, don't even tell me. That kid, Andreas, he's a menace. I am going to talk to this kid forever. I said, Carter, he goes, yeah. I said, Carter, this kid's a menace. What are we going to do about this kid, Andreas? I just don't know. And Carter's like, I don't know either. I'm like, wow, you think maybe Andreas has got some of his own emotional problems maybe? And Carter's like, yeah, I think he does. And just like that, by entering Carter's world exactly where he was, I paced him. In fact, I even got angrier than where he was. I paced him and I paced him and then I let him exactly where I wanted to go, which used to a place of calmness, a place of understanding, compassion almost. And because I entered his world to the right spot and moved him where I wanted to go slowly and deliberately, bam, I got him and he ended up exactly where I want to be and where he should be, which is a state of calm and a state of compassion. That's a classic example of pacing and leading at the highest possible level. Now understand this with pacing and leading, there's two types of pacing and leading. There's pace, pace, lead, which you might have heard about somewhere else. Well, you know, say, you know, matching where they are, matching where they are, and then moving where you want to go. That's one way. And then there's pace, pace, lead on steroids, ninja style, where I'm saying you actually get right in there and you really mirror them. It's one of those things where if it's not exactly right, it works like a charm. And if it's done half heartedly, it hardly works at all. So when we're talking about pace, pace, lead, I want you to understand is that emotional state, the body language, the tone out, you have to mirror your subject, right? You match them, pace them, you pace them, and then you slowly lead them where you want them to go. And if you do it effectively, the results are truly staggering. Pacing and leading is something that is, let's just say it's incredibly effective when it's done correctly and totally worth us when it's done ineffectively. So let me just go through pacing and leading one more time and explain it on a bit deeper level. So step one about pacing and leading is you enter a prospect's world where they are. So if you're speaking at a certain voice and timber and at a certain rate of speech, if you're breathing at a certain level, deep breather or shallow breather, if you're standing a certain way, at a certain level of certainty in your voice or excitement, then when I start to influence you, my goal is to influence you. When I first make my move, I don't start by sounding five times as enthusiastic. I start off and I enter your world of certainty. Let's use certainty for this particular instance. I enter your world of certainty where you are. So if you're at a seven, let's say you're at a seven of certainty, which would mean, yeah, I think that's a pretty good idea. Versus a 10 would be, oh my God, that's the best idea since it's widespread, right? So like, yeah, I think that's a pretty good idea. No, slightly more than ambivalent. So I wouldn't say to you, if you said to me that's a pretty good idea, I wouldn't say exactly because you would unconsciously say, he's not hearing me, we break rapport. So instead, if you say, it sounds pretty good, I say exactly. And I enter your world and say, and let me say this, Victoria, the true beauty of the program is XYZ ABC. Now that was in the same level of certainty as we started. In fact, four years ago, this other company came in and they were also at XYZ and then they went to ABC 123. Now the key with that was is after they went to ABC, now I'm a little bit higher. They went here and then afterwards when that happened, two other companies just like them, they got it all forward and went around and they all made massive money. So when we look that in terms of what you're doing, don't you see what I see? I'm making up the words that matter. Don't you see what I see? You see all four of those things totally wrap up into this one amazing opportunity? You follow what I'm saying and you'll say, oh no, yeah, and you'll jump in and you'll now match me. You'll try to, because I paced you, I paced you and then I started leading you in the direction of where you want, I wanted you to go. That's pacing and leading at a very high level. So it's got to be, you enter their world where they are, you pace, you pace and you lead. Now I made up some funny words there, it's this and that and that and this to illustrate a point that the words are really not the most important thing here because we're talking about moving somebody emotionally using tonality. But of course the words matter too. If you're spewing out nonsense, the process is going to say, guy, this guy is full of nonsense, right? So the words have to be perfect as well and we'll discuss that when we talk about how to write amazing scripts, scripts that actually close people versus scripts that repel people. We'll get to that later but in terms of right now I want to focus on the tonal aspects of what we're doing here and how by shifting your own tone through this process of pacing someone and then pacing them again and then actually leading them to where you want them to go, bam, you move them through a place of emotional certainty and they don't even know why. So watch again, let me show it to you very slowly this time. You say to somebody, does my product make sense to you? Do you like my product? And again you wouldn't use those exact words when I'm just illustrating a point and they say, yeah, it sounds pretty good. Now think about that. They say, yeah, it sounds pretty good. On a scale of certainty, now watch us draw it out for a second here. Let's say we have uncertainty over here, which means, no, I hate your product. And then here we have, let's call this absolute certainty, whereas, oh my god, your product is the best thing in the world, right? Obviously as an influencer, we're looking to move people from uncertainty to absolute certainty. So let's call one would be absolute uncertainty. They hate your product, it's the worst thing in the world. When anybody ever buy here, no, they're not going to buy if they hate your product. On the other end of the continuum, we have absolute certainty. When they love your product, they think it's the best thing since sliced bread. Now here's the deal. There's actually two types of absolute certainty. You have logical absolute certainty. What I mean by that is they've actually made the mental calculations, they've figured out all the features, the benefits, how much it costs, and they weigh it out and say, yeah, this really does make sense. That's logical certainty. Then there's something even more profound which is called emotional certainty. And that means for some gut reason, they just say to themselves, yeah, I got to have it. This thing's unbelievable. Oh my god, got to have it. That's emotional certainty. As an influencer, using the straight line and the reason we're able to close anybody who's closeable is because as we move them down the straight line from the open to the close, we're not just building up logical certainty and we're not just building up emotional certainty. We're building up both at the same time. And what happens is it's the words that move them logically and it's your tone of voice that moves them emotionally. So by combining the right words and then ending on the process of pacing and leading, we're able to move them both logically and emotionally to a place of absolute certainty. And when we get into that spot where they're just like, they're like, yeah, wow, it makes sense logically, but they're also like, hmm, I got to have this now. This is just, wow, yeah, this is, wow, they can't even verbalize. That's emotional certainty. Then your closing rate skyrockets through the roof. So again, this process of pacing and leading, you pace, you pace, and then you lead them where you want them to go. And you create that emotional certainty that at the end of the day is what closes the sale.
